lens.included_activities: Government establishments managing and operating adult or juvenile prisons, jails, detention centers, penitentiaries, and other correctional institutions for confinement, correction, and rehabilitation.
lens.excluded_activities: Private facilities-support contractors, private prison vendors classified outside public administration, probation and parole offices, courts, police, software vendors, captive supplier units, and non-operating financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Tax-funded and appropriated public correctional operations serving courts and jurisdictions rather than commercial external customers; there is no coherent lower-middle-market private-firm revenue model inside this NAICS code.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default outsourced private-firm lens is inapplicable because Census defines 922140 as government establishments. The packet retains the statutory population and marks private-firm transfer and commercial-retention constructs missing rather than inventing a proxy industry.

a.bridge: BLS reports 351,420 correctional officers and jailers in May 2023 and describes duties dominated by guarding, supervision, searches, escorts, safety inspection, counts, and report writing. I translate this occupation-level duty evidence into a minority wage-weighted AI-exposed task share across correctional institutions, concentrated in reports, logs, scheduling, records, video-alert triage, translation, and administrative case support. This proxy may overstate substitution because safety-critical human presence and due process require review, or understate it where surveillance and records consume large staff time.
a.rationale: AI can reduce information-processing and monitoring burden, but custody, de-escalation, searches, transport, emergency response, clinical judgment, and accountable use of force remain human and physical.
a.change_evidence: Institution-level wage-weighted staffing and time-use data, paired with validated AI trials measuring false alerts, report time, staffing impact, incidents, and human review.
caveats: Occupation-level data do not provide the full institution task mix.
High-stakes civil-rights and safety constraints sharply limit substitution even where technical exposure exists.

rho.rationale: Records, drafting, scheduling, translation, and supervised video triage can be implemented within five years, but procurement, cybersecurity, bias, due process, unions, privacy, safety validation, and legacy systems slow operational use.
rho.change_evidence: Audited state and federal correctional AI deployments reporting accuracy by subgroup, incidents, appeals, human-review burden, procurement cycle, and realized labor hours.
caveats: Capability in administrative workflows does not imply authorization for custody or disciplinary decisions.

e.rationale: The statutory industry consists of government establishments, and the frozen private-firm count is explicitly unavailable; there is no defensible denominator of lower-middle-market firms eligible under the lens.
e.change_evidence: A separately defined private-contractor industry and firm universe outside NAICS 922140 would be required, which would constitute a different screen.
caveats: Private prison and facilities contractors exist but are classified outside this government code and cannot be imported into the denominator.

s5.rationale: Government correctional institutions do not undergo qualifying private-company control transfers, and no eligible-firm denominator exists.
s5.change_evidence: A re-scoped private-operator population outside 922140 with observed control transactions would be needed.
caveats: Agency reorganizations, contract awards, closures, and privatizations are not equivalent to private-firm control transfers under the defined construct.

q.rationale: Appropriated government operations do not retain an implemented gross benefit through commercial pricing, renewal repricing, or customer competition in the sense defined by q.
q.change_evidence: A re-scoped fee-for-service private correctional contractor population with contract pricing and renewal data would be necessary.
caveats: Budget retention rules vary by jurisdiction, but they are not commercial value capture.

d5.bridge: BJS reports the state and federal prison population increased 2% to 1,254,200 in 2023, while the federal population then decreased about 1% to 154,093 in 2024. I translate these recent mixed population movements into roughly stable five-year constant-quality institutional demand. Prisoner counts omit local-jail and juvenile-service intensity and can overstate capacity demand if diversion expands or understate it if crowding, healthcare, and rehabilitation standards raise service per person.
d5.rationale: Court-ordered custody creates persistent demand, but sentencing policy, crime, diversion, demographics, immigration detention, capacity, and jurisdiction budgets can move populations in either direction.
d5.change_evidence: Federal, state, local-jail, and juvenile five-year population and capacity forecasts by jurisdiction, adjusted for mandated staffing and service standards.
caveats: Two annual observations are not a structural forecast.
Custody population is only a proxy for constant-quality institution output.

o.rationale: Remaining confinement, safety, rehabilitation, healthcare coordination, and lawful custody require an accountable public institution. Software can assist workflows but cannot assume legal custody or institutional accountability.
o.change_evidence: Statutory or operational evidence that noninstitutional or fully automated models replace correctional custody for material populations.
caveats: Diversion and decarceration affect d5, not the operator share of remaining custody demand.

narrative.executive_view: Correctional institutions are durable, labor-intensive public operations with administrative AI opportunities, but they are not an eligible private-firm roll-up population. The missing labor-share, firm-count, transfer, and commercial-retention constructs are fundamental rather than diligence gaps that can be filled within this NAICS code.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist incident-report drafting, records search, translation, scheduling, staffing forecasts, mail or communications review, education support, maintenance planning, and human-supervised video alerts. Custody, searches, transport, de-escalation, emergency response, treatment, discipline, and use-of-force decisions remain high-stakes human functions.
narrative.value_capture: Productivity may improve public budgets or service quality, but there is no commercial customer-pricing mechanism or private operating-firm EBITDA retention within the statutory government population.
narrative.firm_availability: Census explicitly limits the industry to government establishments. Private contractors and facilities operators are outside the code, so no defensible lower-middle-market eligible-firm universe or control-transfer rate exists here.
narrative.demand_durability: Recent prison counts are mixed but broadly stable, and lawful custody remains institutionally required. Sentencing reform, diversion, crime, immigration policy, juvenile policy, and capacity decisions can materially change jurisdiction-level volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Beyond lens ineligibility, AI use faces civil-rights, bias, privacy, security, due-process, labor, procurement, false-alert, and safety risks. Institutional outcomes cannot be reduced to staffing savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Large administrative and monitoring workloads offer supervised AI-assistance opportunities within persistent public custody operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: The NAICS population is governmental and has no eligible private-firm acquisition or commercial-retention construct.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Correctional Institutions
sources.supports: Census defines 922140 as government establishments managing and operating prisons, jails, detention centers, penitentiaries, and other correctional institutions.

sources.title: Occupational Employment and Wages, May 2023: Correctional Officers and Jailers
sources.supports: BLS reports 351,420 correctional officers and jailers and describes guarding prisoners as the core duty; state and local government employ 181,650 and 140,200 respectively.

sources.title: Prisoners in 2023: Statistical Tables
sources.supports: BJS reports the U.S. state and federal prison population was 1,254,200 at yearend 2023, up 24,100 or 2% from 2022.

sources.title: Federal Prisoner Statistics Collected Under the First Step Act, 2025
sources.supports: BJS reports the federal prison population decreased about 1% from 155,972 in 2023 to 154,093 at yearend 2024.
